posts.name: Mark Maddox
posts.cooked:
As a retirement gift for a burghead I was wondering what DRC would be ready to drink now. I recognize that’s a broad ask but I’m not a burg guy so would have no idea where to look. Any help is appreciated!

Also I guess a caveat would be that the bottle is able to be sourced and sent to TX.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
I’d imagine 1949 RC would be ready to drink.

Do you have a price point?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: Price? Particular vineyard? Narrow it down.

posts.name: Alex Valdes
posts.cooked: Lots of bottles are ready to drink in different ways. Some of the recent vintages are drinking very well now. For a more mature experience, I’ve found wines across the 80s, 90s and even early 2000s that are drinking really well. Broadly, I would say the 96s are not ready and the 09 and 10s are not open. The 2000 and 2001s are close depending on what you are opening. I can walk you through what I’ve had recently if you want to discuss privately.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: There’s probably not a bottle ready under $3000 and likely more. Maybe a DRC Marc de Bourgogne?

posts.name: Greg Pierce
posts.cooked:
www.estatewinebrokers.com (based in Austin) has a number of DRC’s of varying vintages/vineyards. Not sure whether their prices are good/bad/indifferent. Not sure what provenance these bottles have. I’m not sure of their shipping policies – I live in Austin and have picked up wines I bought for myself. They have, however, shipped wines I bought as gifts to Austin addresses on my behalf.

As indicated, I have purchased wine from them (not any DRCs, however). I have been satisfied with the wines I purchased and the service.

posts.name: Fred Bower
posts.cooked:
I drank several 1992 DRC because they were cheap. It was an early drinking vintage. I would consider the Romanee St. Vivant possibly past prime at this point. I like the recommendation for 2000 and 2001 but have not had any first-hand experiences in awhile. I expect 2002 is also showing nicely too. With 2018 being a solar vintage, you might have a great young DRC experience with it. Pricing makes me shudder but I have fond memories of tasting young or at-release DRC wines.

Happy hunting!
fred

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked: There was a thread not long ago (like within the last year) with somebody wanting to get one for a milestone birthday having never had a DRC wine before. That thread had a lot of replies…

posts.name: Fred Bower
posts.cooked:
I looked at CT values for my holdings and note a distortion in that Echezeaux is running at about 45-50% of the price of La Tache. That should not be. Based upon this, I’d avoid Echezeaux as a “value play” buy. Buy in the middle or reach for a La Tache. I have a soft spot for Echezeaux. If I were offered a full exchange of all of my DRC Echezeaux for La Tache at the market rates (without the premium tax in and out of auction), I’d consider it a very good upgrade.

Michael Chang that seems to be a regrettable moment. I hope it comes back to that at some point. I’d much prefer to be able to afford to drink it than to have to sell.

Cheers,
fred

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
It’s getting mighty late in the year to ship a $3000 to $5000 bottle of wine to [or at least within] Texas.

You need to hurry, and/or be prepared to pay for UPS Next Day by 10:00AM [which could cost another several hundred dollars].

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
According to this thread, the 2018s are ready to drink, and Pogos has a 2018 Richebourg.

That gibes with my impression, from back in the day, before COVID [when you could actually get out of the house and go to wine tastings]; that throughout France the 2018s had yuge fruit esters.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: 2000 La Tache is a good choice. An '07 Richebourg this week was right in the zone.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: P.S: '04 La Tache is really good. Transends the vintage.

According to this thread, the 2018s are ready to drink, and Pogos has a 2018 Richebourg.

That gibes with my impression, from back in the day, before COVID [when you could actually get out of the house and go to wine tastings]; that throughout France the 2018s had yuge fruit esters.

I had the ‘18 Petit Monts a week ago and it was a stunningly beautiful, complete, and open wine. Haven’t revisited the rest of the lineup in more than a year, but it’s reasonable to think they will go through a shut-down phase at some point before too long (there is some debate at the moment as to whether the ‘17s, which we’re drinking exceptionally well and open in their youth, may be starting this now).

It was a great night. I’m still waiting to come down from the high before I write down my recollection of the evening. Here is a teaser in the meantime.

IMG_0370.jpeg4032×3024 2.77 MB

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Wow 18 Trapet; was that drinking well? I haven’t opened any of my young trapet because I assumed it wouldn’t be ready; 12 was super tight last winter.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
Wow 18 Trapet; was that drinking well? I haven’t opened any of my young trapet because I assumed it wouldn’t be ready; 12 was super tight last winter.

I Coravin’d a few ounce pour from this bottle a couple of months ago. Let’s just say it tasted like '18. I pulled the cork on this a couple of hours before we left our house. I figured I would blind my friend on this, knowing he has an outstanding palate. I wrapped it in foil and put an old Groth reserve cork in it. When we arrived, I suggested we drink after the Richebourg. And it went something like this: “Has this been Coravin’d before I get that Coravin taste?” “Yes” I said. “This is clearly a Burgundy but it’s very modern and more like a CA pinot, is this '18?” “Yes” I said. “Ok, I am getting Gevrey, but clearly top quality, probably Chambertin, could be Trapet.” Damn that was quick.

This may turn out to be a fine wine 40 years down the road, but I think '19 will be more my style in my lifetime.
